titleStmt.title: Letter from Major Francis Fletcher-Vane to Hanna Sheehy Skeffington, 1 August 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Francis Fletcher-Vane

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Letter from Major Francis Fletcher-Vane (1861-1934) to Hanna Sheehy Skeffington (1877-1946) regarding the political fallout in London in the aftermath of Francis Sheehy Skeffington's death. According to Vane there is a sense of panic among the British administration as they attempt to control the extent of the damage done to their reputation in the wake of Bowen-Colthurt's actions. Before signing off Vane discusses the possibility of going to America as he is quite well known there and would not mind a change.
Major Sir Francis Patrick Fletcher-Vane was a British officer in command of Portobello Barracks. When he learned about the activities of Captain J.C.Bowen-Colthurst during Easter Week he reported the matter to his superiors but, sensing a cover-up, he went to London and reported it directly to Lord Kitchener and Maurice Bonham Carter, Private Secretary to the Prime Minister. Although Bowen-Colthurst was court-martialled and found guilty, Vane was 'retired' from the military for his actions.
Hanna Sheehy Skeffington (1877-1946), suffragette, nationalist, language teacher, was the founder of the Irish Women’s Franchise League and a founding member of the Irish Women Workers’ Union. She was the widow of Francis Sheehy-Skeffington who was summarily executed on 26 April 1916. She was active during the Rising, bringing food to the Volunteers in the G.P.O. and the College of Surgeons. Four days passed before she found out what had happened to her husband, Francis (1878-1916), and it wasn't until almost two weeks later that the full details of his execution emerged.

msIdentifier.collection: Sheehy Skeffington Papers, Collection List No. 47; Ms 33,608/1-4

correspAction.placeName: National Liberal Club, Whitehall Place, London, England

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
National Liberal Club
Whitehall Palace S. W. 1st August 1916 My Dear Mrs Skeffington,
We had the first skirmish of
my particular side of your War to day
as you will have seen by your papers.
I have never seen any people so tied
up as thet were — so entirely futile.
I had a most amusing interview with
the Military Secretary yesterday —
and he was so nervous that he
accused me of stealing his letters and then apologised. It is almost
pitiable how completely they put themselves
in the wrong and how wonderfully
surprised they are where the World
realises their wrong doing. They become
pained and insulted by such a realisation.
Well in a Country in Shed it is necessary
to day fudges co altogether exorbitant
salaries to insure (or try to) impartial
judgments no one need be surprised.
A Driude fudge of wheat gets £700 aavast
our £5000!! I asked many members where our inquiry
would come off and all said very
soon. within a couple of weeks.
I am glad to think the </gap?
Freeman was right. It was horribly
badly edited. But the meaning is
clear enough. Would you send me
any commentaries upon it. I have
the most rotten paper cutting agencies
please — Alas I am to blame. I find in all these
exciting times the Pax Britannica
did not go off. Tomorrow it will
be dispatched. I expect tomorrow in Dublin there will
be a one ask in the Nationalist Papers
over the matter in the House Today.
Please tell me what they say.
I have half a mind to go to America
for a town, for Ireland — I have
often been asked to go there to lecture
and, if they do not want me to fight,
it would be a pleasant change. In a way I am rather well known there
as a the founder of the World Scout
Movement and through my ancestor
for Harry who is a past hero. My best love to Owen — and
my homage to yourself — and Lady O's
love. Yours sincerely
Francis Patrick Vane
of Hutton.
